repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Problem:
Currently, only some file systems on linux (among them: Btrfs, ext2, ext3, and ext4) have full support of accessing the type of a `dirent` entry. In the cases where it is not supported` uv.fs_scandir_next` returns `nil` for the entry type. This causes downstream functions (such as M.del) and logic that rely on a type to be specified to behave incorrectly.

Solution:
fall back to `uv.fs_lstat` when `etype` is `nil`, and if `fs_lstat` fails fall back to "unknown". It should be noted that it is not possible to handle `nil` as we would handle "unknown" because there is overlap in code logic for when directories aren't installed.

`fs_lstat` must also be used instead of `fs_stat` in order to prevent incorrectly assigning the entry type of symlinks.

This PR should close #39612

comment_data.body: `uv.fs_scandir_next()` is used twice in this function. So it is probably a good idea to extract this in a helper and add a brief comment explaining why it is needed.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -206,10 +206,22 @@ function M.dir(path, opts)
       if not fs then
         return
       end
-      return uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+
+      local name, etype = uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+      if not name then
+        return
+      end
+
+      if etype == nil then
+        local stat = vim.uv.fs_lstat(M.joinpath(path, name))
+        etype = stat and stat.type or 'unknown'
+      end
+
+      return name, etype

@@ -206,10 +206,22 @@ function M.dir(path, opts)
       if not fs then
         return
       end
-      return uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+
+      local name, etype = uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+      if not name then
+        return
+      end
+
+      if etype == nil then
+        local stat = vim.uv.fs_lstat(M.joinpath(path, name))
+        etype = stat and stat.type or 'unknown'
+      end
+
+      return name, etype
     end
   end
 
+

```suggestion
        -- Use lstat to avoid ...
        local stat = vim.uv.fs_lstat(M.joinpath(path, name))
```

comment_data.body:
Can you elaborate? I dont see how `fs_scandir_next` is being used twice in this function. The fs_lstat fall back is independent of `fs_scandir` and `fs_scandir_next`, and from what I understand that pattern is used to separate the stream of the path and returning the matches in an iterator-like style. 

I also think it may be cleaner to keep it here as the code logic is simple and easy to follow.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -206,10 +206,22 @@ function M.dir(path, opts)
       if not fs then
         return
       end
-      return uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+
+      local name, etype = uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+      if not name then
+        return
+      end
+
+      if etype == nil then
+        local stat = vim.uv.fs_lstat(M.joinpath(path, name))
+        etype = stat and stat.type or 'unknown'
+      end
+
+      return name, etype

comment_data.body:
By "this function" I meant `vim.fs.dir()` (missed that returned value is a function itself).

There are two places where `uv.fs_scandir_next` is used: [one](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/6780e78b0981f52aec9c4ef96356d3fef3742c9a/runtime/lua/vim/fs.lua#L209) and [two](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/6780e78b0981f52aec9c4ef96356d3fef3742c9a/runtime/lua/vim/fs.lua#L222). Both of them (in theory) can return `nil` second value.

It is arguably more explicit to write and use a custom `fs_scandir_next()` wrapper that ensures the documented returned value ("Returns: `string, string` or `nil` or `fail`").

       if not fs then
         return

-      return uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)

+      if not name then

+      end

+      if etype == nil then
+        local stat = vim.uv.fs_lstat(M.joinpath(path, name))
+        etype = stat and stat.type or 'unknown'
+      end
+
+      return name, etype

comment_data.body:
Thanks for the clarification. I completely forgot about it in `coroutine` .... You are also absolutely correct, a helper is indeed the correct approach for this. It would avoid a repetitive code and a necessary check every time `fs_scandir_next` is called for future development that ends up needing to using it. What do you think of the following:

```lua
--- Wrapper around `uv.fs_scandir_next()` that ensures a file type is returned.
--- If `uv.fs_scandir_next()` returns a `nil` type for an existing entry, 
--- this helper falls back to `fs_lstat` to resolve it. if fs_lstat fails it 
--- defaults to 'unknown'.
---
--- @param fs uv.uv_fs_t The scan handle.
--- @param path string The directory path being scanned.
--- @return string? name The entry name. Nil when the scan is complete.
--- @return string? type The entry type guaranteed if a name is present or an error message.
function fallback_scandir_next(fs, path)
   -- use lstat instead of stat to avoid descending into a symlink entry as a directory/file
    local name, etype = uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)               
    if not name then             
      return   
    end                                                                             
    if etype == nil then                 
      local stat = vim.uv.fs_lstat(M.joinpath(path, name))                          
      -- Workaround #39612 https://github.com/luvit/luv/issues/660
      etype = stat and stat.type or 'unknown'
    end 
    return name, etype
  end

```
On the same note; where do you think this helper should live?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -206,10 +206,22 @@ function M.dir(path, opts)
       if not fs then
         return
       end
-      return uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+
+      local name, etype = uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+      if not name then
+        return
+      end
+
+      if etype == nil then
+        local stat = vim.uv.fs_lstat(M.joinpath(path, name))
+        etype = stat and stat.type or 'unknown'
+      end
+
+      return name, etype

comment_data.body:
Overall looks okay, but overly verbose. Only the first annotation line needs to be present, other annotations are a bit too much. Also:
- Name it `fs_scandir_next`, as it is a very thin wrapper around `uv.fs_scandir_next`.
- Use `uv.fs_lstate` and add comment for why it is used instead of `uv.fs_stat` directly above it.

> On the same note; where do you think this helper should live?

Just above `M.dir()`.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -206,10 +206,22 @@ function M.dir(path, opts)
       if not fs then

       end
-      return uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+

+        etype = stat and stat.type or 'unknown'

+

comment_data.body:
Please review my latest [commit](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39749/changes/40f363d780d8201751d80e3f85c69d7bc76cb003).

Also some basic testing and double checking  I made a change to what I had proposed for the wrapper function:
- Instead of doing:
```lua
if not name then             
      return   
    end
```
I slightly modified the structure and removed the explicit `return` so it naturally returns nil, etype (which is also still nil). 

I made sure to also pass in the second parameter for both instances where this wrapper function is called.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -206,10 +206,22 @@ function M.dir(path, opts)
       if not fs then
         return
       end
-      return uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+
+      local name, etype = uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+      if not name then
+        return
+      end
+
+      if etype == nil then
+        local stat = vim.uv.fs_lstat(M.joinpath(path, name))
+        etype = stat and stat.type or 'unknown'
+      end
+
+      return name, etype

@@ -163,6 +163,23 @@ function M.joinpath(...)
   return (path:gsub(iswin and '[/\\][/\\]*' or '//+', '/'))
 end
 
+--- Wrapper around `uv.fs_scandir_next()` that ensures a file type is returned.
+---
+--- @param fs uv.uv_fs_t
+--- @param path string
+--- @return string?
+--- @return string?
+function fs_scandir_next(fs, path)

comment_data.body:
1. Please adjust formatting (as described in [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#style)).
2. The previous version with explicit `if not name` check is more robust. The current approach does not cover the potential case of `nil, string` return of `uv.fs_scandir_next`. Yes, it should theoretically not happen, but so does `string, nil` case which this helper is trying to solve.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -163,6 +163,23 @@ function M.joinpath(...)
   return (path:gsub(iswin and '[/\\][/\\]*' or '//+', '/'))
 end
 
+--- Wrapper around `uv.fs_scandir_next()` that ensures a file type is returned.
+---
+--- @param fs uv.uv_fs_t
+--- @param path string
+--- @return string?
+--- @return string?
+function fs_scandir_next(fs, path)
+   -- use uv.fs_lstat instead of uv.fs_stat to avoid descending into a symlink entry as a directory/file
+    local name, etype = uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+    if name and etype == nil then
+      local stat = vim.uv.fs_lstat(M.joinpath(path, name))
+      -- Workaround #39612 https://github.com/luvit/luv/issues/660
+      etype = stat and stat.type or 'unknown'
+    end
+    return name, etype
+  end

@@ -206,7 +223,8 @@ function M.dir(path, opts)
       if not fs then
         return
       end
-      return uv.fs_scandir_next(fs)
+
+      return fs_scandir_next(fs, path)

comment_regular_data.body: I guess it might be hard to test this, but this is one of those times where it's tricky enough that "mocking" is justified. And it will also help us avoid surprises in the future if luv's behavior changes later. Maybe ask an AI to come up with a test, then try to refine it.

> I guess it might be hard to test this, but this is one of those times where it's tricky enough that "mocking" is justified. And it will also help us avoid surprises in the future if luv's behavior changes later. Maybe ask an AI to come up with a test, then try to refine it.

When you say "mocking", are you still referring to a a functional test? like by manually modifying what the function returns in order to test the fallback? Also I guess this depends on your answer to the previous question, but where would that go? under `test/functional/lua/fs_spec.lua`?

> > I guess it might be hard to test this, but this is one of those times where it's tricky enough that "mocking" is justified. And it will also help us avoid surprises in the future if luv's behavior changes later. Maybe ask an AI to come up with a test, then try to refine it.
> 
> When you say "mocking", are you still referring to a a functional test? like by manually modifying what the function returns in order to test the fallback? Also I guess this depends on your answer to the previous question, but where would that go? under `test/functional/lua/fs_spec.lua`?

It seems like we are pivoting to creating a wrapper around `fs_scandir_next` to use... I assume what you are looking for with regards to testing will also change?

> When you say "mocking", are you still referring to a a functional test? like by manually modifying what the function returns in order to test the fallback? Also I guess this depends on your answer to the previous question, but where would that go? under `test/functional/lua/fs_spec.lua`?

using `n.exec_lua` in a functional test, you can override stuff in the Nvim test process. by "mocking" I just mean monkey patching, instead of trying to arrange an actual filesystem failure, since I assume that will be difficult for this case.

> I assume what you are looking for with regards to testing will also change?



not really.

> using `n.exec_lua` in a functional test, you can override stuff in the Nvim test process. by "mocking" I just mean monkey patching, instead of trying to arrange an actual filesystem failure, since I assume that will be difficult for this case.


Here's my shot at this:
```lua
describe('fs_scandir_next fallback', function()
  before_each(function()
    mkdir('testdir')
    write_file('testdir/test.txt', 'test file')
  end)

  after_each(function()
    rmdir('testdir')
  end)

  it('falls back to fs_lstat when uv.fs_scandir_next returns nil type', function()
    local result = n.exec_lua([[
      local orig = vim.uv.fs_scandir_next

      vim.uv.fs_scandir_next = function(fs)
        local name = orig(fs)
        if name then
          return name, nil
        end
        return name
      end

      local out = {}
      for name, etype in vim.fs.dir('testdir') do
        out[name] = etype 
      end

      return out
    ]])

    eq('file', result['test.txt'])
  end)
end)
```

> > yep 👍
> 
> Where should this go?

[This](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/7662001c482d63e1119aafcebc0e13d1201a2805/test/functional/lua/fs_spec.lua#L143-L255) is a test set for `vim.fs.dir()`. So probably [after the currently test case](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/7662001c482d63e1119aafcebc0e13d1201a2805/test/functional/lua/fs_spec.lua#L254).

> [This](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/7662001c482d63e1119aafcebc0e13d1201a2805/test/functional/lua/fs_spec.lua#L143-L255) is a test set for `vim.fs.dir()`. So probably [after the currently test case](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/7662001c482d63e1119aafcebc0e13d1201a2805/test/functional/lua/fs_spec.lua#L254).

Unless I'm missing something this PR is ready to be merged into master, but I'm not sure why the functional test suite on MacOS intel has a failure:
```
FAILED   1 test, listed below:
FAILED   ...Xtest_xdg_terminal/test/functional/terminal/tui_spec.lua @ 1082: TUI split sequences work within 'ttimeoutlen' time
...Xtest_xdg_terminal/test/functional/terminal/tui_spec.lua:1082: Row 1 did not match.
```
Does it have a history of failing sporadically?
Ah yes. The macOS Intel runner sporadically has some timing problems.

labels.description: file metadata/attributes, filenames, path manipulation, VFS

title: fix(vim.fs): fs.dir() may return nil "type" on some filesystems #39749
